upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows a very stable, nearly isothermal lower atmosphere with a lapse rate of only 2.26°C/km from surface to 850mb — far below dry adiabatic (9.8°C/km) and even moist adiabatic (6.5°C/km), indicating a strong low-level inversion that will severely cap thermal development today. The boundary layer heights across all sites are modeled at only 35–420m AGL, confirming thermals will be shallow and weak. Winds are light-moderate from the W/NW at 15 kts at 850mb and increase to 30 kts from 270° at 500mb, suggesting upper-level flow is benign but any mixing into the boundary layer will accelerate the afternoon sea breeze.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the SF Bay Area. Notable METAR anomalies: KHAF reporting sustained NNW winds 19–21 kts gusting 24–29 kts with haze and 5–7SM visibility — strong offshore push at Half Moon Bay coastal corridor. KSFO reporting peak wind 30 kts from 300° at 0042Z. KRHV reporting NW 11 kts at 0147Z with scattered clouds at 10,000 ft. Conditions are pre-dawn for today (Mar 21) so these METARs reflect overnight into early morning state.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — inland valley ridge, NW-facing launch
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin's high launch at 1750 ft works on thermal cycles rising from the warm Santa Clara Valley floor, with the afternoon NW sea breeze providing orographic lift against the ridge. Best conditions are W-NW winds 8–18 kts with strong thermal development mid-afternoon. On light-wind days, thermals are the primary mechanism.
sites.todaySetup: The deciding factor today is the extremely stable atmosphere: a lapse rate of just 2.26°C/km means thermals will be weak and capped, with model-predicted boundary layer heights topping out at only 350–385m AGL — well below the 1750 ft launch elevation in energy terms. Morning is calm and disorganized (winds 2–5.5 kts, shifting direction erratically from S through E through N), which rules out useful ridge soaring. The afternoon NW sea breeze does arrive around 1300–1700 local, building to 6–7 kts at 10m with 7–9 kts at 80m from 300–320°, which is at the low end for reliable ridge lift here. Critically, cloud cover reaches 91–98% from noon through 3 PM, which will suppress what little thermal energy exists. The 4–5 PM window sees clouds clear (7% cover) and winds settle at 6.9–7.2 kts NW, offering the only marginally workable ridge soaring window, but with shallow BL heights (345–385m) and stable air, any lift will be weak and scratchy. Not a rewarding flying day from the high launch.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds light and variable 2–5.5 kts shifting S→E→NE; BL height only 35–265m. No usable lift — thermals not firing, ridge lift absent.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind slowly shifts to NW but only 2.6–5.2 kts at 10m; 91–98% cloud cover from noon onward kills solar heating. Sea breeze front arriving but too weak and overcast to generate useful lift.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window: 4–6 PM once clouds clear, winds NW 7 kts. Ridge lift marginal at best given stable airmass. Suitable only for experienced pilots comfortable with light, scratchy conditions.

verdict.hazards: Stable airmass, cloud cover suppressing thermals noon–3 PM, low BL height (350–385m), erratic morning wind direction, scratchy lift only

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — lower training hill, NW-facing slope
sites.howItWorks: The 600 ft training hill at Ed Levin works on gentle valley thermals and light NW sea breeze ridge lift. Ideal for P2 pilots in smooth, light conditions with W-NW winds 5–12 kts. Lower elevation means faster sea breeze exposure and less exposure to strong upper winds.
sites.todaySetup: The stable atmosphere and low boundary layer heights (35–410m) are the dominant story here, just as at the high launch. Morning winds are light and variable (2–3 kts, multi-directional), offering no ridge lift and only marginal thermal potential. The sea breeze arrives through early afternoon, building NW winds to 6–7.5 kts by 1300–1500 local — workable for light soaring, but cloud cover of 92–98% from noon through 3 PM will suppress solar heating and thermal quality. The best window opens 4–6 PM when clouds clear and NW winds hold 7.5–7.7 kts at 10m with 7.7–9 kts at 80m, which is actually the cleanest sea breeze flow of the day at this level. The 600 ft hill is more sheltered from upper-level turbulence and can deliver smooth sledders and brief soaring in the afternoon sea breeze even on marginal days.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 2–3 kts, light and variable from W/N/NW/SW — no organized flow. Sledder conditions at best, no soaring potential.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: NW sea breeze building to 5.7–6.7 kts by 1–2 PM, but 92–98% cloud cover suppresses thermals. Possible light ridge soaring in afternoon sea breeze if wind direction holds.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: 4–6 PM clearest window: clouds thin to 12%, NW winds 7.5–7.7 kts steady. Smooth sea breeze ridge soaring possible for P2 pilots in the 4–5 PM window.

verdict.hazards: Noon–3 PM overcast kills thermals, light and variable morning winds, stable lapse rate, CAPE near zero

sites.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
sites.howItWorks: The 300 ft beginner hill is used for ground handling, short flights, and learning. Requires very light and consistent W-NW winds, 5–10 kts. Primarily a training venue — sledder flights with occasional soaring in ideal sea breeze.
sites.todaySetup: Today's stable atmosphere keeps thermal energy negligible at this elevation. Morning winds start at 3–4.6 kts from W/NW but shift to SW/W with some variability (242–307°), which is usable for the W-facing slope, though not ideal. Midday sees the same overcast (93–98% cloud cover noon–3 PM) that affects the upper launches, and winds shift more westerly at 5–8 kts. The afternoon sea breeze window from 1400–1900 produces the best conditions here: NW winds 7.4–8.2 kts at 10m from 293–317°, which is solid for the 300 ft hill's aspect. With skies clearing to 20% cloud cover by 4 PM, this could support smooth short soaring flights. Wind speed is on the higher end for a true beginner hill — P2s with minimal experience should be cautious.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 3–4.6 kts W/NW, light but reasonably on-gradient. Suitable for ground handling and short sledder training flights.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds build to 5–7.7 kts from W/WNW but 93–98% overcast suppresses any thermal help. Wind speed borderline for beginners, especially if gusty.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: 3–5 PM best window: NW 7.4–8.2 kts, skies clearing to 20% by 4 PM. Good sea breeze flow for the slope but on the higher end for P2 beginners — requires supervision.

verdict.hazards: Winds 7–8 kts may be high for true beginners; noon–3 PM overcast; stable air; wind speed on upper limit for 300 ft training hill

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal sea cliff, W/NW-facing ridge site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a pure ridge-soaring site driven by onshore NW/W ocean wind hitting the coastal bluffs. Works best with NW winds 12–22 kts at the cliff; too light and you can't stay up, too strong and the rotor behind the ridge becomes dangerous. Marine layer burn-off timing is critical — fog can close the site. KHAF METAR and buoy data are key indicators.
sites.todaySetup: The coastal conditions today are the determining factor, and they are mixed. The KHAF METAR is reporting sustained NNW winds of 19–21 kts gusting 24–29 kts — this is strong, well above ideal, and indicates a powerful pressure gradient driving NW onshore flow. The Half Moon Bay buoy (46012) shows SST of 12.9°C with 2.9m waves at 9-second period, and winds at 23 kts from 310°, confirming the offshore marine layer is active and the sea is rough. The GFS/HRRR models at Mussel Rock show morning winds of only 4–7 kts at 10m, which likely reflects sheltering or a model resolution artifact — the buoy and KHAF data suggest actual coastal winds will be substantially stronger in the morning, potentially in the 15–20 kt range. Afternoon sees model winds building to 7–9.5 kts at 10m, 9–13.5 kts at 80m, with 79–82% cloud cover intermittently. The SST of 12.9°C is cold, which means the marine layer will be robust and cloud cover may be variable and low. Conditions are likely soarable for an experienced P3 pilot in the mid-morning window after any early morning cloud/haze clears, but the strong gusts (24–29 kts at nearby KHAF) make this a site for experts only today.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Model shows 2–6 kts onshore but KHAF/buoy data suggests actual coastal winds significantly stronger (15–21 kts NNW). Variable cloud 11–82%. Assess on-site before launching — could be excellent or too strong/gusty.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Models show 6–7 kts W/NW at 10m, 6–8.5 kts at 80m. Cloud cover variable (19–79%). If KHAF gusts moderate from overnight levels, this could be the cleanest soaring window.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds building to 7–9.5 kts at 10m, 9–13.5 kts at 80m from W/NW. Intermittent clouds 2–82%. Ridge lift strengthening but gustiness increasing — rotor behind the bluff becomes a concern above 18 kts.

verdict.bestWindow: 11 AM – 2 PM (assess KHAF gusts on-site first)
verdict.wind: 8–10 kts W/NW (model); coastal gusts may be 20–25 kts per KHAF
verdict.hazards: KHAF reporting 19–21 kts gusting 24–29 kts — actual coastal winds likely much stronger than model; cold SST 12.9°C sustains marine layer; variable cloud cover; rotor risk if gusts exceed 18–20 kts; haze reducing visibility to 5–7SM

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.locationDescriptor: Fremont — inland ridge/peak, SW-facing thermal and ridge site
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak relies on a combination of valley thermals from the warm Fremont/Tri-Valley floor and afternoon NW/W sea breeze ridge lift along the Diablo Range. Ideal conditions are NW 8–15 kts with active thermals. The site is sensitive to the sea breeze front timing and tends to be overdone late afternoon if the gradient is strong.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak shares essentially the same atmospheric setup as Ed Levin given similar inland valley positioning — the model data is nearly identical between the two sites. The deeply stable lapse rate (2.26°C/km) is the primary constraint: thermals will be capped and weak, with BL heights of only 35–385m AGL through the afternoon. Morning winds are light and variable (2–5.5 kts, shifting from S through E and back to NW) offering no organized lift. The sea breeze arrives through the afternoon, bringing NW 5–7 kts by 2–4 PM with 80m winds reaching 7.3 kts — marginally workable but not inspiring. Cloud cover hits 91–98% from noon through 3 PM, exactly the window when thermals would otherwise peak, guaranteeing a suppressed thermal day. The 4–6 PM window offers the best chance: clouds clear (7% cover), NW 6.9–7.2 kts, with a brief CAPE flicker of 10 J/kg at 4 PM — negligible but the only hint of instability all day. Mission Peak at 2600 ft summit means you need reliable lift to make it work; today's conditions are borderline.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind light and variable 2–5.5 kts, shifting S→E→NE. BL height 35–265m. No usable lift of any kind — stay on the ground.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 91–98% cloud cover through noon to 3 PM kills thermal development. Sea breeze arrives (NW 2.6–5.2 kts) but too weak and overcast to support soaring flight at the peak.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: 4–6 PM best window: clouds clear to 7%, NW 6.9–7.2 kts, weak sea breeze ridge lift possible. Marginal — pilots should have conservative exit strategies given stable air and fading light.

verdict.hazards: Extremely stable lapse rate caps thermals all day; 91–98% overcast noon–3 PM; light variable morning winds; BL height below 400m throughout; fading light if waiting for best window

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Sunday looks more active but complex. Morning starts with 100% overcast and NE winds 9.4–12 kts at 10m, gusting to 21 kts at 80m — an unusual offshore flow pattern suggesting a pressure pattern change. Tomorrow.io shows NE gusts to 16–20 kts by afternoon. Conditions may become flyable mid-afternoon (12–2 PM) as winds shift NW at 5.8–6.7 kts and clouds thin to near-zero, but the afternoon see 100% cloud return by 4–6 PM. The morning offshore flow is concerning and unusual — not a straightforward flying day. Best window if any: 12–3 PM in the clearing. P3+ only given morning instability.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Sunday morning offshore NE at 14.5 kts gusting 22.9 kts at 80m (HRRR) — too strong and wrong direction for this launch. Afternoon NW transition 6.9–7.3 kts at 10m (12–4 PM) looks cleaner with near-zero cloud cover midday. Tomorrow.io shows gusts to 16–18.5 kts afternoon — punchy for this site. Best window 12–2 PM in the clearing, P2+ with caution.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Sunday's morning offshore component at 3.7 kts NNW/NW at surface with 11 kts at 80m (100% overcast) keeps early morning grounded. Afternoon 12–4 PM sees W/NW 5–7.6 kts with good visibility and near-zero cloud — solid afternoon sea breeze window for the 300 ft hill. Gusts may be punchy (Tomorrow.io 16 kts) by 2 PM. Reasonable P2 afternoon with supervision.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Sunday shows W/WNW 6.8–9.7 kts at 10m with 80m winds 7.6–11.2 kts, building through the afternoon. Morning cloud 86% then clears. Afternoon (12–4 PM) looks like the classic Mussel Rock sea breeze setup — winds W 7–10 kts at 10m, consistent with good ridge soaring. Tomorrow.io shows 5–8 kt gusts (much lighter than HRRR), suggesting models disagree; weight HRRR. 16–18% precip chance at 4–6 PM warrants monitoring. Overall a better day than today for Mussel Rock — P2+ afternoon window likely.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Sunday morning: 100% overcast with strong NE flow at 80m (21.3 kts). By midday the NE component fades, clouds clear (7% by noon), and NW 5.8–6.7 kts develops for the afternoon. Same pattern as Ed Levin — midday clearing window 12–3 PM could be flyable with light sea breeze ridge lift. Tomorrow.io shows NE gusts 11–12 kts by afternoon which is unusual for this site — worth watching. P3 afternoon.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Monday (March 23) shows a nicer pattern: morning starts light (2 kts from 163°/319°), clouds build to 96–99% by 10 AM then clear rapidly. Afternoon NW 5–7.4 kts with BL heights reaching 430–500m — slightly better than today. 4–6 PM window: 7.4 kts NW, 0% cloud, 500m BL. Better thermal day with more insolation if morning cloud clears as modeled. P2+ afternoon.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Monday similar pattern to high launch — morning cloud then clear afternoon. NW 5–7.4 kts by 2–4 PM, BL 370–512m. Cleaner day overall with 0% cloud 2–6 PM. Good P2 afternoon window.

day2.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
day2.analysis: Monday afternoon looks good: NW 5.2–7.4 kts at 10m, 0% cloud, BL up to 530m by 4 PM. Sea breeze arrives cleanly and holds through evening. Best day of the four-day window for this training hill.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Monday: morning light (5.7–6.8 kts W) with 100% cloud at 10 AM then clearing. Afternoon NW 5.1–6.9 kts, 4–6% cloud, W/WNW 4.5–7.6 kts at 80m. Moderate, consistent sea breeze — looks like a solid P2+ Mussel Rock day if morning marine layer clears as modeled.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Monday shows light and variable morning (2 kts from 117°), 96% overcast at 10 AM, then clearing. Afternoon NW transition: 3.7–4.4 kts at 10m but 6.8–9.9 kts at 80m — notable vertical wind shear. Thermals possible with better insolation but upper winds may be punchy relative to surface. P3 day.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: Tuesday (March 24): calm morning (1.2 kts), building NW sea breeze afternoon to 7.5–8.5 kts at 10m with BL heights reaching 515–760m — the best thermal ceiling of the four-day period. 64% cloud cover at 4 PM may limit the window. 12–4 PM window looks promising with 0–5% cloud. Potential for the strongest thermal day of the outlook.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Tuesday mirrors the 1750 ft forecast closely — BL 515–760m afternoon, NW 5.5–8.5 kts. Low cloud probability midday. Could be the best P2 flying day of the stretch if clouds stay minimal.

day3.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
day3.analysis: Tuesday same pattern: calm morning, building NW 5.5–8.5 kts afternoon, excellent BL heights for the area (515–760m). 64% cloud at 4 PM may limit afternoon session. Best 12–4 PM window.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: Tuesday: calm morning (1.5 kts), building W/NW 5.9–8.3 kts midday through afternoon with BL 230–375m. Afternoon 80m winds 7.6–10 kts. Looks like a moderate Mussel Rock day — 20–33% cloud cover may be present. P2+ afternoon.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Tuesday shows interesting setup: near-calm morning, building W/WNW at 10m (2.7–5.2 kts) but strong at 80m (9–11.3 kts) — significant low-level shear. BL heights 500–725m suggest good thermal potential. However the shear between surface and 80m warrants caution. P3+ recommended.

watchlist: KHAF reporting 19–21 kts gusting 24–29 kts overnight into morning — actual coastal winds at Mussel Rock likely well above model values; assess on-site before launching
Noon–3 PM overcast (91–98% cloud cover) at all Ed Levin elevations today will kill thermal development during the prime solar heating window
Stable lapse rate (2.26°C/km surface to 850mb) suppresses thermals at all inland sites — do not expect classic spring thermal flying today
Sunday morning unusual NE/N offshore flow (9–14 kts surface, 21 kts at 80m) at Ed Levin — monitor for rotor potential and pilot drift risk before NW sea breeze reestablishes
Cold SST 12.9°C at Half Moon Bay buoy with 2.9m/9s swell — robust marine layer may delay coastal clearing and limit Mussel Rock morning windows this weekend
Tuesday (March 24) shows the highest BL heights of the period (760m) and lightest morning winds — best potential thermal day of the outlook if cloud cover permits
tldr: Today is a marginal day across all Bay Area sites — a brutally stable airmass (lapse rate 2.26°C/km) caps thermals, and noon–3 PM overcast at the inland sites kills the best solar window. Your best bet is Ed Levin 1750/600 ft or Mission Peak in the 4–6 PM sea breeze window (~7 kts NW), or Mussel Rock if you're P3+ and the coastal gusts (currently 24–29 kts at KHAF) moderate by midday. Don't rush out early — patience is required today.
